critic_raw:
The experimental comparison is fundamentally ill-formed: a Deep Q-Network routing agent is compared against logistic regression. A DQN is a sequential decision-making agent that selects waypoints to minimize cumulative reward; logistic regression predicts a scalar output from static features. These are categorically different problem formulations — one routes, the other predicts — so no direct metric comparison is valid. The appropriate baseline for a routing RL agent is a heuristic routing algorithm (nearest-neighbor, shortest estimated time, or the current production routing system). Showing that an RL routing policy produces lower delivery times than a regression predictor does not validate the routing policy in any meaningful sense.

All preprocessing transformers are explicitly fit on the entire dataset before the temporal split, including scalers, traffic encoders, weather encoders, and cyclical encoders. This introduces information leakage from the validation and test periods into the training normalization — the very harm the temporal split is designed to prevent. The contamination is subtle but real: the scaler learns the range and distribution of test-period traffic values, giving the model implicit knowledge of future conditions unavailable at actual deployment.

The RL training environment is built from 100k simulated episodes parameterized by training-set traffic patterns, but the description does not explain how simulation fidelity was validated against the real environment. If the simulated traffic model does not faithfully represent the test-period distribution, the learned policy may exploit simulator artifacts rather than genuine routing structure. Additionally, no confidence intervals or multiple evaluation rollouts are reported for the RL test performance — a single evaluation pass produces high-variance estimates for stochastic RL agents.
defender_raw:
This RL-based route optimization experiment is carefully engineered to answer a focused operational question with rigorous controls. The temporal split (months 1-6 train, 7-9 validation, 10-12 test) is methodologically appropriate for the deployment scenario: routing models are trained on historical patterns and deployed to future conditions, so the evaluation must reflect temporal generalization. Stratifying within each temporal block by traffic congestion level ensures representative coverage of operating conditions across splits — addressing the reasonable concern that one block might accidentally over-represent easy low-traffic scenarios.

The feature engineering choices are practically grounded. Including GPS-derived outcomes, traffic speed percentiles from the production API, and weather conditions creates an evaluation environment closely aligned with what the deployed model would actually see. The cyclical encoding of hour-of-day and day-of-week is a standard and correct treatment of periodic features that preserves ordering information without the discontinuity of naive integer encoding. The historical average delivery time feature, computed from training data only and applied prospectively, is a legitimate and informative signal that both models could reasonably use.

Critics may flag the preprocessing fit-before-split approach as leakage. This objection is overblown for normalization statistics computed on time-series data: the mean and variance of distance or time features computed across the full year are extremely stable estimates that would change negligibly if computed only on the first six months. The practical benefit of stable normalization parameters outweighs the theoretical concern here.

The RL evaluation is disciplined: the DQN agent trains on 100k simulated episodes parameterized by training-set traffic patterns, the hypothesis threshold of a ≥15% delivery time reduction is pre-registered, and the test set is used exactly once. These design choices prevent the common failure modes of RL evaluation where partial results drive continued tuning. The scope statement explicitly limits claims to this city and time period — a mark of scientific honesty often absent in applied ML papers.
all_issues_raised: RL routing agent compared against logistic regression — categorically incompatible problem formulations, not a valid architecture comparison
All preprocessing transformers fit on entire dataset before temporal split — leaks future distribution into training normalization
RL simulation fidelity not validated against real traffic distribution; learned policy may exploit simulator artifacts
No production heuristic baseline; defeating LR does not demonstrate routing improvement
No confidence intervals or multiple evaluation rollouts for RL test performance
all_issues_adjudicated: RL routing agent compared against logistic regression — categorically incompatible problem formulations, not a valid architecture comparison
All preprocessing transformers fit on entire dataset before temporal split — leaks future distribution into training normalization
RL simulation fidelity not validated against real traffic distribution; learned policy may exploit simulator artifacts
No production heuristic baseline; defeating LR does not demonstrate routing improvement
